title: The Macro Minute: The shutdown is ending; why aren’t risk assets appreciating?

Happy Tuesday out there team 42 your
skipper here Darius Dell to present our
macro minute for Tuesday November 11th
2025 as always we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lit up
morning note so let's hop right in
today's macro question is the shutdown
is ending why aren't risk assets
appreciating the short answer is because
as long as we've maintained the US
federal government shutdown is a
sideshow compared to the pile Fed's
latest policy mistake terms of the
analytical nuance the historic 42-day US
shutdown is poised to conclude as early
as Wednesday after the Senate approved a
temporary spending uh uh temporary
spending bill funding most federal
operations through January 30th and
select agencies through September 30th.
House Speaker Mike Johnson expects swift
passage in his chamber. Once enacted,
the measure heads to President Trump,
who has already endorsed after it
strongest session since May, MAG7 stocks
are dragging indices lower as SoftBank
Group offloaded its entire 5.8 8 billion
Nvidia stake to fund other AI ventures,
sending the chipmaker down 3% Tuesday.
Treasury futures climbed and the dollar
weakened after ADP research data
signaled a sharper labor market
deterioration in the second half of
October with traders turning to private
data sets as the ongoing government
shutdown delays official economic
releases. According to ADP Research, US
companies cut an average of 11,250 jobs
per week during the four weeks ending
October 25th. This compares to his
latest monthly report which showed ADP
private payrolls rising 42,000 in
October after back-to-back declines.
Elsewhere, Bloomberg data show corporate
mentions of economic slowdown in
synonyms during earnings calls have
fallen to their lowest level since 2007
in Q3. Similarly, media coverage mirrors
this optimism as references to the
economic slowdown in Bloomberg news
articles dropped to their lowest levels
since June 2021. This positive data
represents the top of the K-shaped US
economy and stands in stark contrast to
the October NFIB small business optimism
survey results which featured a further
deterioration in this critical subset of
the US economy. Recall that small
businesses account for three-fifths to
twothirds of employment growth. This
suggests the incremental deterioration
in small business sales, profit, and
hiring expectations is likely to weigh
on subsequent jobs reports and signal to
the tardy pow fed that it has made yet
another policy mistake in a tenure
littered with him. For what it's worth,
in 2013, when the prior shutdown
affected the jobs report, the government
reopened on October 17th and released
the September employment data 5 days
later. That suggests the September
employ September jobs report is likely
to be reported sometime next week. As
always, members of our global investor
community can trust that our
institutional grade risk management
overlays Kiss and Dr. Mo will help our
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
So, as always, we'll wrap up with a
question from our global investor
community here in our 42 micro
dashboard. So, this one's titled, "Where
will the 7.5 trillion sitting in money
market funds go as the rate cuts
continue?" Says, "Hi, Skipper. Do you
have a fundamental point of view on the
7.5 trillion currently sitting in money
market funds? Specifically, do you
expect this will draw down as the
cutting cycle continues? And if so,
where is that money most likely to go?"
So, rather than answer that with words,
let me just go to my Bloomberg charts.
Got a chart here at graph number 868
uh out of 1,800 1,800 charts in four and
a half years. That's what it takes to
stay on the right side of market risk,
folks. Um so this chart here shows the
the blue line in the top panel shows ICI
money market fund assets 7.5 uh trillion
dollars. Uh the red line shows uh blue
mer uh stock market cap US equity stock
market capitalization uh and the bottom
panel just shows the the ratio of of
money market fund assets to uh the uh US
equity market capitalization. And so uh
couple points I'll make on this. Uh one
we are at a historically uh not
historically low but cyclically low
level cyclically and structurally low
level in terms of the ratio of money
market fund assets to US equity market
cap. uh specifically we're in the uh
25th percentile of of daily data going
back to uh 2003 which is as far back as
we can have the time series for the uh
equity market capitalization data. Uh
second the most important point I would
make is the blue line in the in the top
panel which is the uh nominal value for
the uh money market fund assets. There's
only two times in this in this time
series and in fact I actually have data
going back to the 70s for this and can
confirm the only time the money market
fund assets declines on a on a durable
basis is coming out of recession. So uh
you typically see a mad dash for cash
heading into the recession and then
ultimately that cash is redeployed um
after the recession. You know this is
Wall Street uh you know doing a good a
reasonably decent enough job uh uh
managing risk. Uh so the key takeaway
here is you should not expect the cash
on the sidelines um the money on the
sidelines to ever sort of be used as
fodder to capitalize asset markets when
they're at their highs. They will
eventually be used at capitalized asset
markets when the markets are at their
lows at their postc crash lows. But the
reality is it's not a bull cap. Money
market fund assets historically is not a
bull catalyst um you know when markets
are at their highs. It's more likely
that we see an acceleration in the
accumulation of money market fund assets
particularly once the subsequent bare
market starts. Uh that's obviously when
when the markets are done pricing in our
bullish paradigm see theme which we've
maintained uh since April. So we'll wrap
it up there. Terry Stell here presenting
our macro minute for Tuesday, November
11, 2025. Best of luck out there today.
We'll catch you back here tomorrow. And
happy Veterans Day to all of our
military family and friends on Team 42.
We appreciate you. Thank you for your
service. And uh God bless each and every
one of you. Cheers.
